source_name: Colonel G. B. Malleson, History of the Indian Mutiny of 1857-8, Vol. V (1897), Book 14 Chapter 3
revision_note: Newly added with 8 verified chunks under public domain (2026-07-23). Source: ibiblio.org 'British Raj' collection (https://www.ibiblio.org/britishraj/KayeMalleson5/bk14ch03.html), a transcription of Malleson's History of the Indian Mutiny of 1857-8 (1897), which the ibiblio.org project states is presumed public domain under both US and UK copyright rules. This is a British military-history account written from the perspective of the colonial force that defeated her; it is used here (rather than a modern biography) specifically because it is a documentary, contemporaneous public-domain source, and the chunks include the author's own closing tribute to her as a national heroine to balance the framing. Two candidate English-language sources were reviewed and rejected: Michael White's 'Lachmi Bai Rani of Jhansi, the Jeanne d'Arc of India' (1901, archive.org identifier lachmibairaniofj00whitiala) is public domain by age but is catalogued by its own publisher/library metadata as a Fiction/novelised work, not a documentary source, and was rejected on content-integrity grounds; 'Rani Lakshmi Bai Of Jhansi' by Shyam Narain Sinha (archive.org in.ernet.dli.2015.99215) is dated 1980 and is a modern work not reliably in the public domain despite a DLI 'In Public Domain' metadata tag, and was rejected as a modern copyrighted biography per project policy. D. B. Parasnis's original 1894 biography is in Marathi and was not used since no verified public-domain English translation of it could be located.

content.text: After the fall of Jhansi and the defeat at Kalpi in May 1858, the Rani of Jhansi fled with Rao Sahib toward Gopalpur, where Tantia Topi joined them. Their affairs seemed desperate: they had lost central India, the Sagar and Narbada territories, and Bundelkhand, and enemy columns were closing in from every side.
content.source_name: Colonel G. B. Malleson, History of the Indian Mutiny of 1857-8, Vol. V (1897)

content.text: Malleson records that of the confederate leaders, the Rani of Jhansi alone "possessed the genius, the daring, the despair necessary for the conception of great deeds," and that the desperate plan to march on and seize the fortress-city of Gwaliar from Maharajah Sindhia -- appealing to his troops' national feeling and turning it into a stronghold of resistance -- was almost certainly hers: "The conjecture, then, almost amounts to certainty that the desperate remedy... was suggested and pressed upon her comrades by the daring Rani of Jhansi."
content.source_name: Colonel G. B. Malleson, History of the Indian Mutiny of 1857-8, Vol. V (1897)

content.text: After the plan succeeded and Gwaliar was taken, "the command of the bulk of the troops, encamped outside the city, was entrusted to the Rani of Jhansi." Malleson notes that no one, "not even Sir Hugh Rose, had imagined the height of daring to which the Rani of Jhansi would carry her audacious plans" -- so bold that the British at first believed the march on Gwaliar must be a trap rather than a genuine attempt to seize it.
content.source_name: Colonel G. B. Malleson, History of the Indian Mutiny of 1857-8, Vol. V (1897)

content.text: At the Battle of Kotah-ki-Sarai (18 June 1858), "clad in the attire of a man and mounted on horseback, the Rani of Jhansi might have been seen animating her troops throughout the day." When the British 8th Hussars finally charged and broke the rebel line, she boldly fronted the British horsemen even as her own comrades gave way around her.
content.source_name: Colonel G. B. Malleson, History of the Indian Mutiny of 1857-8, Vol. V (1897)

content.text: As she tried to escape with the others, her horse stumbled crossing a canal near the cantonment. "A hussar close upon her track, ignorant of her sex and her rank, cut her down. She fell to rise no more." Malleson records that that night her devoted followers, determined that the English should not boast of capturing her even dead, cremated her body themselves.
content.source_name: Colonel G. B. Malleson, History of the Indian Mutiny of 1857-8, Vol. V (1897)

content.text: Malleson writes of the aftermath: "Though one bitter enemy, the Rani of Jhansi, had disappeared," the resistance in central India continued under Tantia Topi for months afterward -- a testament, in the historian's own framing, to how central her leadership and resolve had been to the wider 1857-58 uprising in the region.
content.source_name: Colonel G. B. Malleson, History of the Indian Mutiny of 1857-8, Vol. V (1897)

content.text: Malleson, writing as a British military historian, nonetheless closes his account with a tribute: "Whatever her faults in British eyes may have been, her countrymen will ever believe that she was driven by ill-treatment into rebellion; that her cause was a righteous cause; and that the treatment she received at the hands of Lord Dalhousie was one of the main causes of the disaffection in Bundelkhand and Central India in 1857-8. To them she will always be a heroine."
content.source_name: Colonel G. B. Malleson, History of the Indian Mutiny of 1857-8, Vol. V (1897)

content.text: This entry compiles verified passages from Colonel G. B. Malleson's History of the Indian Mutiny of 1857-8, Vol. V (1897), Book 14, Chapter 3, 'Sir Hugh Rose and Gwaliar', as transcribed and hosted by the ibiblio.org 'British Raj' public-domain text collection. The work is a British colonial military history and its perspective should be read with that context in mind; it is used here as a documentary, contemporaneous, rights-clear primary source rather than a modern biography, per project sourcing policy.
content.source_name: Colonel G. B. Malleson, History of the Indian Mutiny of 1857-8, Vol. V (1897)
